agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "Proposed change to auto-delete delivery records after 90 days, down from 7 years. Framed as infrastructure optimization but carries severe legal, regulatory, and contractual implications in EU/UK logistics markets. This is NOT an infrastructure change — it is a fundamental business policy change with compliance risk.",
    "bounded_context": "Data Retention / Delivery Records Management",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "Delivery Record", "definition": "The complete transactional record of a parcel/shipment from pickup to final delivery, including proof of delivery, timestamps, chain of custody, and associated metadata." },
      { "term": "Proof of Delivery (POD)", "definition": "Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient — signatures, photos, GPS coordinates, timestamps. Legally required for dispute resolution and insurance claims." },
      { "term": "Data Retention Policy", "definition": "The rules governing how long business records are stored before deletion. In logistics, driven by legal statute, contractual obligation, and operational need — not storage cost." },
      { "term": "Chain of Custody", "definition": "The documented trail of who handled a shipment at each stage. Critical for liability determination in loss/damage claims." },
      { "term": "Limitation Period", "definition": "The legal window during which a party can bring a claim. In the UK this is typically 6 years (Limitation Act 1980); in many EU jurisdictions 3-10 years depending on claim type." },
      { "term": "B2B Compliance Reporting", "definition": "The use of delivery data by business customers for their own regulatory filings, tax records, audit trails, and contractual compliance — often with retention requirements of 6-10 years." }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery records must be retained for at least the statutory limitation period applicable in the jurisdiction of delivery, to defend against legal claims.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If records are deleted before the limitation period expires, the company cannot defend itself in court against delivery disputes, fraud claims, or liability suits. This is a legal survival constraint, not a policy preference.",
      "testable_assertion": "No delivery record is deleted while the applicable limitation period for its delivery jurisdiction has not expired."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "UK deliveries require a minimum 6-year retention period under the Limitation Act 1980 for contract and tort claims.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Statutory requirement. A court case filed in year 5 would require the delivery record as evidence. Deletion at 90 days makes the company defenseless.",
      "testable_assertion": "All UK delivery records are retained for a minimum of 6 years from the delivery date."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "EU deliveries must comply with member-state limitation periods, which range from 3 years (e.g., Germany for standard claims) to 10 years (e.g., France for contractual claims).",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Varies by jurisdiction but all exceed 90 days by a wide margin. Deleting at 90 days violates every EU member state's limitation period.",
      "testable_assertion": "All EU delivery records are retained for a minimum period matching the applicable member-state limitation law."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "Tax-relevant delivery records (invoices, commercial documentation) must be retained per tax authority requirements — typically 6-10 years across EU/UK.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Tax authorities can audit years after the fact. Missing records during a tax audit results in penalties, estimated assessments, and potential fraud investigations.",
      "testable_assertion": "Delivery records containing tax-relevant data are retained for the applicable tax retention period (minimum 6 years UK, varies by EU member state)."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "B2B customers who rely on delivery data for their own compliance reporting must have access to records for the duration specified in their service agreements.",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a contractual obligation that varies by customer agreement, but deleting data that customers depend on for their compliance would constitute a breach of contract and destroy customer trust.",
      "testable_assertion": "Delivery records for B2B customers are retained at least as long as the longest contractual retention obligation in their service agreement."
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "GDPR requires that personal data not be retained longer than necessary for its purpose — but the 'purpose' includes legal defense, contractual obligation, and regulatory compliance, which justify multi-year retention.",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "GDPR Article 17(3)(e) explicitly exempts data needed for legal claims from the right to erasure. The current 7-year policy likely has a valid legal basis. Reducing to 90 days does not improve GDPR compliance — it destroys legally-required records.",
      "testable_assertion": "Data retention periods are justified by documented legal bases under GDPR Article 6, and records needed for legal defense under Article 17(3)(e) are not deleted prematurely."
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Customer files a delivery dispute claim 4 years after delivery", "expected_behavior": "The delivery record, including proof of delivery, must be retrievable to defend the claim. Under a 90-day policy, the record would have been destroyed 3+ years prior — company loses by default." },
    { "scenario": "Tax authority audits delivery invoices from 3 years ago", "expected_behavior": "All delivery and invoice records must be produced. Under a 90-day policy, company faces penalties for missing records and potential fraud investigation." },
    { "scenario": "B2B customer runs annual compliance report requiring 5 years of delivery history", "expected_behavior": "Data must be available per contractual SLA. Under 90-day policy, customer cannot complete their own compliance obligations — breach of contract and customer churn." },
    { "scenario": "Insurance claim for high-value goods lost in transit, filed 18 months after delivery", "expected_behavior": "Chain of custody records must be available to support or contest the claim. 90-day deletion destroys all evidence." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory investigation into delivery practices spanning multiple years", "expected_behavior": "Company must produce historical records. Inability to do so due to auto-deletion could be viewed as spoliation of evidence." },
    { "scenario": "GDPR subject access request for delivery from 2 years ago", "expected_behavior": "If data was deleted at 90 days, company cannot fulfill the request — but also cannot demonstrate it had a lawful basis for deletion that early, creating a GDPR compliance paradox." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "SEVERE. Exposure to undefendable legal claims (6-year window in UK alone), tax authority penalties for missing records, potential insurance claim losses without proof of delivery, and breach-of-contract liability with B2B customers. Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single lost lawsuit or tax penalty. A single undefended high-value delivery claim could exceed years of storage savings.",
    "operational": "DESTRUCTIVE. B2B customers relying on historical delivery data for compliance reporting would lose access, breaking their workflows. Customer support cannot resolve disputes older than 90 days. Finance/accounting loses audit trail. Legal team loses evidence base.",
    "user": "B2C consumers lose ability to reference past deliveries for warranty claims, returns, or disputes after 90 days. B2B customers lose compliance-critical data they depend on, likely forcing them to migrate to a competitor that retains records appropriately.",
    "risk": "CRITICAL. Legal risk: inability to defend claims within limitation periods. Regulatory risk: HMRC/EU tax authority penalties. Contractual risk: breach of B2B service agreements. Reputational risk: perceived as unreliable data custodian. Compliance risk: potential GDPR violation paradox where deletion itself lacks lawful basis. This change transforms a storage cost into an existential legal liability.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance Team", "impact": "Loses ability to defend the company in any delivery dispute older than 90 days. Directly exposed to regulatory penalties." },
      { "group": "B2B Logistics Customers", "impact": "Lose access to delivery data required for their own compliance reporting. Likely breach of SLA/contract. High churn risk." },
      { "group": "B2C Consumers", "impact": "Lose proof of delivery for warranty, insurance, and dispute purposes beyond 90 days." },
      { "group": "Finance/Tax Team", "impact": "Cannot produce records for tax audits. Exposed to penalties and estimated tax assessments." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Cannot investigate or resolve any delivery issue older than 90 days." },
      { "group": "Insurance Partners", "impact": "Cannot process or validate claims without historical delivery evidence." },
      { "group": "Executive/Board", "impact": "Bears ultimate liability for regulatory non-compliance and destruction of legally-required records." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 92,
    "reasoning": "The legal retention requirements for delivery records in EU/UK are well-established statutory obligations (UK Limitation Act 1980, EU member-state civil codes, VAT directives). The 90-day proposal violates every applicable legal minimum by a factor of 10-40x. The business rules here are not ambiguous — they are settled law. Minor uncertainty only around specific EU member-state variation and exact contractual terms with B2B customers.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "UK 6-year limitation period requirement",
      "Tax record retention requirements (6-10 years)",
      "B2B contractual dependency on historical data",
      "GDPR does NOT require 90-day deletion — legal defense is an explicit exemption",
      "This is NOT an infrastructure-only change"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Exact retention periods per EU member state (varies by country and claim type)",
      "Specific contractual retention obligations in B2B agreements (would need contract review)",
      "Whether current 7-year policy has formal legal basis documentation (should exist but unverified)"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "The platform processes physical delivery of goods (not purely digital services)",
      "B2B customers have contractual access to historical delivery data",
      "The current 7-year policy was established with legal input and has a documented basis",
      "Delivery records include proof of delivery, timestamps, and transaction details"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "contradiction",
      "description": "The engineering team frames this as 'no business logic changes' — this is fundamentally incorrect. Data retention for delivery records IS business logic. It encodes legal obligations, contractual commitments, and regulatory compliance requirements. Deleting legally-required records is not infrastructure optimization — it is destruction of evidence and breach of statutory duty. This framing must be corrected before any further analysis or implementation.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "B2B Customers", "Finance/Tax", "Executive/Board"],
      "options": [
        "A: Reject the 90-day proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention",
        "B: Implement tiered retention — archive cold data to cheaper storage after 90 days but retain for full legal period",
        "C: Engage legal counsel to determine minimum retention per jurisdiction and implement jurisdiction-aware retention policies"
      ],
      "recommendation": "Option B or C. If the goal is cost reduction, move records to cold/archive storage (S3 Glacier, etc.) after 90 days — do NOT delete them. This achieves 80-90% of the storage cost savings without any legal risk. Option C adds jurisdiction-aware minimums for GDPR data minimization compliance. Under NO circumstances should records be deleted at 90 days."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": [
    { "violation": "90-day deletion violates UK Limitation Act 1980 which requires records available for 6-year claim window", "business_rule_broken": "BR-2" },
    { "violation": "90-day deletion violates EU member-state limitation periods (3-10 years depending on jurisdiction)", "business_rule_broken": "BR-3" },
    { "violation": "90-day deletion destroys tax-relevant records required for 6-10 year retention by tax authorities", "business_rule_broken": "BR-4" },
    { "violation": "90-day deletion breaches B2B customer contractual obligations for compliance data access", "business_rule_broken": "BR-5" },
    { "violation": "Framing this as 'no business logic changes' is factually incorrect and dangerous — data retention policy IS business logic encoding legal obligations", "business_rule_broken": "BR-1" }
  ]
}
